_meta.status: CANDIDATE — not yet promoted. Verified inline by lead 2026-07-17 (research agent failed twice on API session-limit + 529 overload). Meets the strict bar: full transcript fetched and read this session, key lines quoted, corroborating synopses, debunk-exposure checked (nil). Evidence log: evidence/TV-008_verification.md.
_meta.note: This is 1 of the 3 replacement Tier 1 pairs. 2 more still needed to reach the pilot's 10 conflict items (or amend the prereg pilot size to what we have).

items.event: Delivers cab driver Arleen's baby in the taxi
items.lure_rationale: The schema is 'the doctor delivers the baby,' and Frasier Crane is THE doctor of the show (title character, 'Dr. Frasier Crane', a psychiatrist). 'Which Crane delivered the baby?' pattern-completes to the marquee psychiatrist son. In fact Martin, the blue-collar retired cop, delivers it because he has the practical skill, while Frasier and Niles are useless (Frasier is shoved aside; Niles is knocked out cold). INTRA-SOURCE lure: Niles falsely claims 'skills I honed in medical school'; Frasier undercuts it ('Niles ran down to a falafel stand for a pot of hot water'). The wrong binding has in-source support; the correct binding is the comedic reversal.
items.ground_truth_evidence: VERIFIED 2026-07-17 against full transcript (The Frasier Archives, kacl780.net S2E4). Arleen to Martin: 'What, are you a doctor too?' Martin: 'No, I'm a retired cop.' / 'I've delivered more than a few babies in my lifetime.' Stage direction: 'Martin pushes Frasier out and gets in himself'; Arleen 'throws up a hand that knocks Niles out cold.' Corroborated by TV Tropes recap + Frasier Wiki. Full log: evidence/TV-008_verification.md.
items.meme_asymmetry_note: Frasier dominates the show's 'the doctor' association (title character). The 'Martin actually delivered it' beat lives only in episode recaps; no misconception genre. Asymmetry favors the lure, stable.

decomposed_facts.question: In Frasier's 'Flour Child', what was Arleen the cab driver's profession-related emergency?
decomposed_facts.answer: She went into labor while driving her cab

decomposed_facts.question: In 'Flour Child', what does Martin say his former profession was when Arleen asks if he's a doctor?
In 'Flour Child', which character actually delivers the baby in the taxi?
In 'Flour Child', what happens to Niles during the delivery?
decomposed_facts.answer: He is knocked out cold by the cab driver's flailing arm

cold_prompts: In the Frasier episode 'Flour Child', which character delivers the cab driver's baby in the taxi?
In Frasier, when a cab driver goes into labor in her taxi, which of the men actually delivers the baby?
items.correct_premise_prompt: Rewatched the Frasier episode where the cab driver gives birth in her taxi — Martin being the one who actually delivers the baby while his sons flail is so good. What does Martin say his qualification is when she asks if he's a doctor?
items.lure_premise_prompt: Rewatched the Frasier episode where the cab driver gives birth in her taxi — Frasier being the one who actually delivers the baby while the others flail is so good. What does Frasier say his qualification is when she asks if he's a doctor?
items.notes: lure_strength medium: the 'Martin delivers, sons useless' reversal is the scene's comedic point, so strong episode knowledge yields the correct answer; the lure operates on schema/protagonist default. Grading note: 'Crane' alone is ambiguous (all three are Cranes) — target keyword is the first name Martin, lure is Frasier; a bare 'Crane' response routes to adjudication.
items.debunk_exposure_check: Searched for the delivery beat framed as a misconception ('people think Frasier/Niles delivered it, actually Martin'). Found only plain recaps stating Martin delivered it; no Mandela-effect / 'actually it was X' genre. Nil exposure.

items.event: Adopts and carries a ten-pound sack of flour as a practice 'baby'
items.lure_rationale: Control: taking a flour-sack parenting exercise deathly seriously is maximally Niles-shaped (fussy, precious, over-invested), and Niles really does it. The archetype answer IS correct. Note idea/act split: Frasier suggests the exercise; Niles adopts and carries the flour baby. Foil for the incorrect-premise condition is Frasier (a realistic wrong guess since he proposed it).
items.ground_truth_evidence: VERIFIED 2026-07-17 against full transcript (kacl780.net S2E4). Frasier proposes the concept ('a ten-pound sack of flour to keep with them for a week as though it were a baby'; 'I wasn't actually suggesting--'); Niles seizes it ('Well, why not?... Frasier, where do you keep the flour?'), then 'Niles sits with his baby', 'I take him everywhere', and to Daphne 'I asked you to baby-sit!' Corroborated by TV Tropes recap + Frasier Wiki. Full log: evidence/TV-008_verification.md.
items.meme_asymmetry_note: The flour-baby is the episode's title bit and firmly Niles-associated; no competing attribution.

decomposed_facts.question: In Frasier's 'Flour Child', which character first suggests the flour-sack parenting exercise?
In 'Flour Child', which character actually adopts and carries the flour baby around?
In 'Flour Child', why does Niles say he wants to try the flour-baby exercise?
decomposed_facts.answer: He is deciding whether he is ready to become a father

decomposed_facts.question: In 'Flour Child', whose flour baby is repeatedly damaged in accidents?

cold_prompts: In the Frasier episode 'Flour Child', which character adopts a sack of flour and carries it around like a baby?
In Frasier, which brother tries the flour-sack 'baby' experiment to see if he's ready to be a father?
items.correct_premise_prompt: In the Frasier flour-baby episode, Niles carrying that sack of flour everywhere like a real child is hilarious. Why does he decide to do the experiment in the first place?
items.lure_premise_prompt: In the Frasier flour-baby episode, Frasier carrying that sack of flour everywhere like a real child is hilarious. Why does he decide to do the experiment in the first place?
items.notes: Control: archetype answer (Niles) is correct. grading_keywords_lure holds the FOIL (Frasier), used in the incorrect-premise condition. 'Crane' alone routes to adjudication (all three share the surname).
items.debunk_exposure_check: N/A for control (schema-congruent). Attribution is uncontested and title-level.
